The White House
January 9, 2026

PROTECTING VENEZUELAN OIL REVENUE: Today, President Donald J. Trump signed an Executive Order declaring a national emergency to safeguard Venezuelan oil revenue held in U.S. Treasury accounts from attachment or judicial process, ensuring these funds are preserved to advance U.S. foreign policy objectives.

The Order blocks any attachment, judgment, decree, lien, execution, garnishment, or other judicial process against Foreign Government Deposit Funds.

Foreign Government Deposit Funds are defined as the Venezuelan oil revenues and diluent sales held in U.S. Treasury accounts.

The Order prohibits transfers or dealings in these funds except as authorized, superseding any prior Orders that might block or regulate them.

The Order affirms the funds are sovereign property of Venezuela held in U.S. custody for governmental and diplomatic purposes, not subject to private claims.

ADVANCING U.S. NATIONAL SECURITY AND FOREIGN POLICY: President Trump is preventing the seizure of Venezuelan oil revenue that could undermine critical U.S. efforts to ensure economic and political stability in Venezuela.

Allowing attachment of these funds would directly jeopardize U.S. objectives, including stemming the influx of illegal aliens and disrupting the flood of illicit narcotics, which has resulted in the death of countless thousands of American citizens.

Loss of control over these revenues would empower malign actors like Iran and Hezbollah while weakening efforts to bring peace, prosperity, and stability to the Venezuelan people and to the Western Hemisphere as a whole.

The funds are held solely for sovereign purposes, and their protection reinforces U.S. compliance with international obligations and supports broader diplomatic objectives.

PUTTING AMERICA FIRST: President Trump has relentlessly confronted threats from the Maduro regime and its enablers to defend American interests and strengthen hemispheric security.

President Trump authorized lethal force against narcoterrorists to stop the flow of deadly drugs into this country.

President Trump ordered a total and complete blockade of all sanctioned oil tankers going in and out of Venezuela.

President Trump offered multiple off ramps to Maduro and his illegitimate regime, but he was clear that the drug trafficking must stop.

Last week, U.S. armed forces conducted an extraordinary military operation in support of law enforcement to bring outlaw dictator Maduro to justice.

It is in the national interest of the United States to be surrounded by safe, secure, and stable countries; The President's actions reestablish deterrence in the Western hemisphere.

By safeguarding these oil revenues, President Trump is promoting stability in Venezuela while advancing America First objectives: stopping illegal immigration, blocking deadly narcotics, and countering malign foreign influence.

PX-1 proves a ground-based extraction force in Caracas but also frames the action as a raid/extraction. Defense submitted two additional official sources: DX-1, a White House Rubio release stating U.S. forces were on the ground about two hours to capture Maduro and that the action was not an invasion or extended military operation; and DX-2, a Department of War transcript describing the action as grabbing an indicted individual wanted by American justice in support of law enforcement. The rebuttal uses those defendant sources as admissions of U.S. forces physically on the ground in Venezuela, nearly 200 Americans downtown Caracas, and a continuing posture until changes were made. No new outside evidence was needed because the issue is the legal effect of existing official-source admissions and plaintiff PX-1.

case_view.proposition:
The event below resolves to YES.

| Property   | Value                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
|------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Event      | The United States Armed Forces conduct a military operation involving ground troops entering Venezuelan sovereign territory with the objective of seizing or holding territory, removing the sitting government, or establishing operational control of a major population center.  |
| Source     | Official statement from the U.S. Department of Defense or the White House confirming deployment of ground forces. Secondary: UN Security Council records.                                                                                                                           |
| Criteria   | Resolves YES if U.S. ground forces enter Venezuelan territory in a combat capacity. Targeted strikes, naval blockades, special operations raids without territorial objectives, and cyber operations resolve NO.                                                                    |
| Edge cases | Capture of a head of state without territorial occupation: NO (separate blueprint for regime change). U.S. forces entering from a third country: YES if troops cross into Venezuelan territory. Proxy forces directed by U.S. command: NO unless U.S. personnel physically present. |
| Fallback   | If DoD and White House issue conflicting statements, UN Security Council records are binding. If no official statement within 72 hours of credible reports, resolution deferred 30 days.                                                                                            |
| Expiration | January 31, 2026. Resolves NO if event has not occurred.                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |

arguments.text:
Council members, the admitted official record satisfies the proposition by a preponderance.

PX-1 is the primary Department of War account. It reports that President Trump announced Maduro’s capture after a “successful overnight joint U.S. military extraction in Venezuela’s capital of Caracas.” It quotes the President saying the U.S. armed forces conducted an extraordinary military operation “in the capital of Venezuela” against a heavily fortified military fortress “in the heart of Caracas.” It then describes Operation Absolute Resolve as a joint military and law-enforcement mission involving U.S. joint forces, including special operations forces, and quotes General Caine on integration of air, ground, space, and maritime operations. Most decisively, PX-1 says more than 150 military aircraft provided cover for the “ground-based extraction force in Caracas.”

Those official statements prove the first elements. U.S. Armed Forces were used; the force included ground personnel; those personnel were physically in Caracas, Venezuela; and they acted in a combat-capable military operation against a fortified target with expected resistance. This is not a proxy-force case, a blockade-only case, a cyber case, or a standoff-strike-only case.

The harder issue is objective, because the proposition excludes capture of a head of state without territorial occupation. PX-1 answers that too. It does not stop at handcuffs and transport. It states that “with Maduro now out of power,” President Trump said the U.S. would “oversee Venezuela” until a safe transition to a legitimate replacement could be identified. It further says the newly sworn Venezuelan vice president was willing to work with the U.S. and that the Secretary of State was working directly on what the U.S. thought necessary for Venezuela. In context, the operation removed the sitting head of government and was paired with U.S. oversight of Venezuela’s transition. That is a qualifying government-removal and operational-control objective, not a bare arrest divorced from political control.

PX-2 is the most adverse official source, and the plaintiff does not hide it. The White House SAP describes “targeted and limited military strikes within Venezuela” in furtherance of a law-enforcement operation to apprehend and transport Maduro and Flores for prosecution. That language supports the defense’s edge-case theory. But PX-2 also confirms that U.S. Armed Forces were engaged in hostilities within or against Venezuela, that Maduro and Flores were beyond ordinary law-enforcement reach at a heavily fortified location, that combat-equipped armed associates and resistance were anticipated, and that U.S. Armed Forces were necessary. It narrows the legal framing; it does not erase the ground force in Caracas or the removal/transition statements in PX-1.

PX-3 corroborates the broader U.S. objective. The White House fact sheet again calls the action an extraordinary military operation and ties post-operation policy to safeguarding Venezuelan sovereign oil revenues held in U.S. custody for governmental and diplomatic purposes, political and economic stability in Venezuela, and U.S. foreign-policy objectives. PX-3 is not the main proof of ground entry, but it supports the inference that the January operation was part of a U.S.-directed transition and control posture after Maduro’s removal.

Provenance favors weight. PX-1 is an official Department of War/Pentagon News publication. PX-2 and PX-3 are official White House materials. They are the very source classes the proposition prioritizes. There is no conflicting admitted UN record. The capture caveat matters, but the record shows more than capture: ground forces in Caracas, a military operation in the capital, Maduro out of power, and U.S. oversight until transition.

The defense will say “limited” and “law enforcement” resolve the case. Those labels do not control where the official operational facts are stronger. A limited operation can still be a qualifying operation if U.S. ground troops enter Venezuelan territory in combat capacity with a government-removal or operational-control objective. On this record, they did. The event occurred before January 31, 2026, and the proposition resolves YES.

Council members, the defense does not need to contest every element. The record now likely proves U.S. personnel entered Caracas in a combat-capable operation before the deadline. But the proposition does not ask whether there was a dramatic raid. It asks whether ground troops entered with a qualifying objective: seizing or holding territory, removing the sitting government, or establishing operational control of a major population center. On that decisive objective element, plaintiff has not carried the burden.

The proposition itself supplies the answer to this fact pattern: “Capture of a head of state without territorial occupation: NO.” It also excludes “special operations raids without territorial objectives.” The admitted official sources repeatedly describe exactly that excluded category.

PX-2, an official White House Statement of Administration Policy, is the cleanest source because it states the administration’s legal and operational framing after the event. It says that on January 3, 2026, the U.S. Armed Forces conducted “targeted and limited military strikes within Venezuela” in furtherance of a “law enforcement operation to apprehend and transport Nicolas Maduro and Cilia Flores to the United States for federal criminal prosecution.” It explains why the military was used: Maduro and Flores were at a heavily fortified location, protected by armed associates, and U.S. forces were necessary to “effectuate their apprehension and transportation.” That proves a capture and transport objective, not occupation, territorial holding, or operational control of Caracas.

DX-1 confirms the same limit from the White House the next day. Secretary Rubio said there was not a war against Venezuela; U.S. forces were on the ground “for about two hours” when they went to capture Maduro; and the action was “not an invasion” and “not an extended military operation.” He also called it “this raid and this capture and this arrest.” Those are not casual labels from an outsider; they are official White House characterizations of the mission’s scope and duration.

DX-2, an official Department of War transcript, matches that account. Secretary Hegseth described nearly 200 Americans going downtown Caracas and grabbing “an indicted individual wanted by American justice in support of law enforcement.” Again, the object is an indicted person, not territory. The presence is brief and mission-specific, not occupation or control.

Plaintiff’s best evidence is PX-1. It does prove a ground-based extraction force in Caracas and contains language that Maduro was “out of power” and that the U.S. would “oversee Venezuela” until a transition. But that language does not cure the missing link. First, PX-1 itself repeatedly frames the operation as an extraction, a raid, and a joint military and law-enforcement mission to capture the Maduros and bring Maduro to justice. Second, the proposition’s carveout would be meaningless if every capture of a head of state automatically became “removing the sitting government.” The carveout requires the council to ask whether there was territorial occupation or a qualifying operational-control objective. PX-1 does not show U.S. troops holding territory or controlling Caracas after the extraction.

Third, the supposed transition language is political aftermath, not proof of a ground-troop objective. PX-1 says Venezuelan Vice President Delcy Rodriguez was “just sworn in” after Maduro’s ouster and willing to work with the U.S. That cuts against the idea that U.S. ground troops established operational control of the government or a population center. At most, it shows U.S. diplomatic pressure and claimed influence after a capture. The proposition requires more.

PX-3 is even further removed. It concerns custody of Venezuelan oil revenues held in U.S. Treasury accounts and broader foreign-policy objectives. It describes the prior operation as an extraordinary military operation “in support of law enforcement.” It may show the United States wanted stability and leverage. It does not show ground troops seizing or holding Venezuelan territory, controlling a major population center, or occupying anything.

Provenance does not rescue plaintiff’s theory. The sources are official, but official sources can prove a NO. Here, the official source chain is consistent on the limiting point: the operation was brief, targeted, law-enforcement-related, and aimed at apprehension and transport for prosecution. There is no admitted UN Security Council record resolving a conflict, and no admitted official statement saying U.S. ground troops occupied Caracas or held Venezuelan territory.

The preponderance question is not whether a raid happened. It did. The question is whether plaintiff proved the qualifying objective despite the proposition’s express NO carveouts. The better reading of the admitted record is that U.S. forces conducted a short-duration armed capture/extraction of Maduro, followed by political and financial measures. That is exactly the edge case the proposition excludes. The event resolves NO.

closings.text:
Council members, the record now gives you a narrow decision. Entry is no longer the real fight. The official sources establish that U.S. personnel were on the ground in Venezuela, in Caracas, before the January 31, 2026 deadline. DX-1 says they were on the ground for about two hours. DX-2 says nearly 200 Americans went downtown Caracas. PX-1 says the mission used a ground-based extraction force in Caracas, covered by more than 150 military aircraft, after months of planning by U.S. joint forces including special operations forces. PX-2 confirms Armed Forces hostilities within Venezuela and the need for military force because ordinary law enforcement could not reach a fortified, armed target.

So the elements of U.S. Armed Forces, ground entry into Venezuelan territory, combat capacity, and timing are met by official U.S. sources. The only serious dispute is objective.

The defense’s best point is the edge case: capture of a head of state without territorial occupation is NO. That point matters, and plaintiff’s case would fail if the record showed only a two-hour arrest and nothing more. But the record does show more.

PX-1, the Department of War account, says Maduro was “now out of power.” It reports that the President said the U.S. would “oversee Venezuela” until a safe transition to a legitimate replacement could be identified. It also reports that the successor was willing to work with the United States to do what the United States thought necessary. Those are not stray policy preferences detached from the operation. They are the official account of what the operation achieved and what the United States would do next: remove the sitting ruler from power and control the transition.

The defense asks you to treat “not an invasion,” “not an extended military operation,” and “law enforcement” as dispositive. The proposition does not use those tests. It does not require a permanent occupation. It does not require a formal invasion. It asks whether ground troops entered in combat capacity with a qualifying objective. A short operation can still have a qualifying objective if it removes the sitting government or establishes operational control of the political transition.

Nor does the edge case swallow the rest of the proposition. It excludes a bare head-of-state capture without territorial occupation or control. It does not exclude a record in which the capture is paired with official statements that the captured ruler is out of power, the United States will oversee the country until transition, and U.S. officials will direct what changes are necessary. On this record, the capture was the means; removal and transition oversight were the qualifying objectives.

Provenance supports using those statements. PX-1 is a Department of War/Pentagon News source, the source class the proposition prioritizes. PX-2 and PX-3 are White House sources. DX-1 and DX-2 are also official sources, and they help the plaintiff on ground entry even while helping the defense on duration. There is no admitted UN record overriding the U.S. official record.

The burden is preponderance, not certainty. The defense has a plausible narrower reading, but the better reading of the whole official record is that U.S. ground forces entered Caracas in combat capacity, removed Maduro from power, and placed Venezuela’s transition under U.S. oversight. That satisfies the event definition. The proposition resolves YES.

Council members, the final record is narrower than the rhetoric around it. The defense accepts that official sources show U.S. personnel entered Caracas in a combat-capable operation before January 31, 2026. The question is whether plaintiff proved the required objective: seizing or holding territory, removing the sitting government, or establishing operational control of a major population center.

That objective element is where plaintiff’s case fails. The proposition itself warns against the shortcut plaintiff asks you to take: capture of a head of state without territorial occupation is NO, and special operations raids without territorial objectives are NO. Those carveouts matter precisely because a dramatic raid can look like regime change unless the record is read carefully.

The most precise official statements of the operation’s purpose point to NO. PX-2 says the U.S. Armed Forces conducted targeted and limited military action in furtherance of a law-enforcement operation to apprehend and transport Maduro and Flores to the United States for federal criminal prosecution. It explains that military force was needed because they were in a fortified location, protected by armed associates, and likely to resist apprehension. That is capture, transport, and prosecution.

DX-1 says the same thing in plainer terms: forces were on the ground for about two hours when they went to capture Maduro; the action was not a war against Venezuela, not an invasion, and not an extended military operation. It calls the event a raid, capture, and arrest. DX-2 likewise describes nearly 200 Americans going downtown Caracas and grabbing an indicted individual wanted by American justice in support of law enforcement. These are official sources from the very source classes the proposition prioritizes. They describe a brief capture mission, not occupation or control.

Plaintiff’s strongest point is PX-1’s language that Maduro was out of power and that the U.S. would oversee Venezuela until a transition. But that language cannot carry the objective element by itself. PX-1 also describes the mission as an extraction, a raid, a joint military and law-enforcement mission to capture the Maduros, and an operation to bring Maduro to justice. It does not say U.S. ground troops stayed in Caracas, held territory, controlled Venezuelan institutions, controlled a major population center, or made a new government effective by military occupation.

The “out of power” inference also proves too much. If capturing a head of state and thereby leaving him unable to rule automatically satisfied “removing the sitting government,” the express edge case would have no work to do. The proposition distinguishes capture from qualifying removal or territorial control. Plaintiff must prove the latter, not just the former.

Nor does PX-3 close the gap. Custody of Venezuelan oil revenues and U.S. foreign-policy objectives may show leverage after the operation. They do not show ground troops seizing or holding Venezuelan territory or exercising operational control over Caracas. And PX-1’s note that Vice President Delcy Rodriguez was sworn in and willing to work with the United States points to political aftermath, not U.S. ground control.

There is no admitted UN Security Council record overriding this reading and no official statement saying U.S. forces occupied Caracas or held territory. The record proves a short, armed, law-enforcement-linked capture and extraction. It does not prove the additional territorial or operational-control objective required for YES.

Because plaintiff bears the burden by a preponderance and the decisive objective element remains unproven, the proposition resolves NO.

exhibits.label: PX-1
exhibits.text:
Trump Announces U.S. Military's Capture of Maduro

Jan. 3, 2026 | By Matthew Olay, Pentagon News

President Donald J. Trump today announced the capture of Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro, following a successful overnight joint U.S. military extraction in Venezuela's capital of Caracas.

Presidential Remarks caption: President Donald J. Trump holds a press conference in Palm Beach, Fla., Jan. 3, 2026, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio, Secretary of War Pete Hegseth, second from right, and Air Force Gen. Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, to discuss Operation Absolute Resolve in Caracas, Venezuela. Credit: White House photo by Molly Riley. VIRIN: 260103-D-D0439-004.

"Last night and early today, at my direction, the United States armed forces conducted an extraordinary military operation in the capital of Venezuela. ... It was an operation against a heavily fortified military fortress in the heart of Caracas to bring outlaw dictator Nicolas Maduro to justice," Trump said during a midday news conference from his Mar-a-Lago residence in Palm Beach, Florida.

The president added that both Maduro and his also-captured wife, Cilia Flores de Maduro, will now face criminal court proceedings tied to a 2020 indictment from the U.S. Department of Justice on multiple federal charges, including narco-terrorism and drug trafficking.

Secretary Hegseth Speaks caption: Secretary of War Pete Hegseth speaks at a press conference in Palm Beach, Fla., Jan. 3, 2026, with President Donald J. Trump, Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Air Force Gen. Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, to discuss Operation Absolute Resolve in Caracas, Venezuela. Credit: White House photo by Molly Riley. VIRIN: 260103-D-D0439-006P.

Earlier in the day, Trump posted a photo to social media showing a blindfolded and handcuffed Nicolas Maduro in custody aboard the U.S. Navy amphibious assault ship USS Iwo Jima.

Labeled Operation Absolute Resolve, the joint military and law enforcement mission to capture the Maduros was the result of months of planning and rehearsal involving U.S. joint forces — including special operations forces — from multiple service branches. The military also networked extensively with multiple U.S. intelligence agencies, according to Air Force Gen. Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff.

"Our interagency work began months ago and built on decades of experience [with] integrating complex air, ground, space and maritime operations. ... We watched, we waited, we prepared, we remained patient and professional. ... This was an audacious operation that only the United States could do," Caine said during the press conference.

President Trump Speaks caption: President Donald J. Trump holds a press conference in Palm Beach, Fla., Jan. 3, 2026, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio, Secretary of War Pete Hegseth and Air Force Gen. Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, to discuss Operation Absolute Resolve in Caracas, Venezuela. Credit: DOW screenshot. VIRIN: 260103-D-D0439-001.

He added that the mission to extract the Maduros — in which there were no U.S. casualties — was so precise that it involved more than 150 military aircraft from all across the Western Hemisphere launching in close coordination to provide cover for the ground-based extraction force in Caracas.

"Words can barely capture the bravery and the power and the precision of this historic operation, a massive joint military and law enforcement raid flawlessly executed by the greatest Americans our country has to offer," Secretary of War Pete Hegseth, who was also in attendance at Mar-a-Lago, told the media.

"What I witnessed last night was sheer guts and grit, gallantry and glory of the American warrior. ... This is about the safety, security, freedom and prosperity of the American people. This is America first; this is peace through strength; and the United States War Department is proud to help deliver it," Hegseth said of the mission.

With Maduro now out of power, Trump said the U.S. will oversee Venezuela until a safe transition to a legitimate replacement for the deposed strongman can be identified.

"We have a group of people running [Venezuela] until such a time it can be put back on track, make a lot of money for [Venezuelans] ... give people a great way of life, and also [reimburse the] people in our country who were forced out of Venezuela," Trump said.

The president added that, while it was his understanding that Venezuelan Vice President Delcy Rodriguez was "just sworn in" [to the presidency] following Maduro's ouster, Rodriguez seemed willing to work with the U.S. to achieve what's best for Venezuelans.

"[Secretary of State] Marco [Rubio] is working on that directly. He just had a conversation with [Rodriguez], and she's essentially willing to do what we think is necessary to make Venezuela great again," Trump said.

The capture of the Maduros represents a culmination of tensions between the U.S. and Venezuela dating as far back as 1998, with the election of President Hugo Chavez, a Venezuelan socialist who espoused anti-U.S. rhetoric and formed close ties with Cuba, Iran and Russia.

Following the death of Chavez in 2013, Nicolas Maduro took over and became increasingly politically hostile toward the U.S. as the years progressed.

Tensions between the two countries increased significantly beginning in September of 2025, when the Trump administration began conducting targeted boat strikes on vessels deemed to be conducting narco-terrorism by attempting to smuggle illegal drugs into the U.S.

In November 2025, the State Department declared Venezuela's state-embedded criminal network Cartel de los Soles — which is alleged to have been headed by Maduro — as a terror organization.

Just prior to that designation, Hegseth said such an action would provide the War Department with "more tools" to provide options to Trump in regard to how to address the narco-terror threat in the region.

"We have a lot of options; we'll make sure we have the authorities to do so, and we will protect the American people," Hegseth said at the time.

exhibits.text:
EXECUTIVE OFFICE OF THE PRESIDENT
OFFICE OF MANAGEMENT AND BUDGET
WASHINGTON, D.C. 20503
January 8, 2026
(Senate)
STATEMENT OF ADMINISTRATION POLICY
S.J. Res. 98 – A Joint Resolution to Direct the Removal of United States Armed Forces from Hostilities within or against Venezuela that have not been Authorized by Congress
(Sen. Kaine, D-VA, and 25 cosponsors)

The Administration strongly opposes passage of S.J. Res. 98, a joint resolution that would direct the removal of United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against Venezuela.

On January 3, 2026, pursuant to a request from the Attorney General, the U.S. Armed Forces conducted targeted and limited military strikes within Venezuela. These strikes were in furtherance of the successful law enforcement operation to apprehend and transport Nicolas Maduro and Cilia Flores to the United States for federal criminal prosecution.

The U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York issued arrest warrants for both individuals based on grand jury indictments for their central roles in a wide-ranging conspiracy. The indictment charged Maduro with engaging in a narco-terrorism conspiracy with narco-terrorist groups, including foreign terrorist organizations, such as FARC, ELN, Tren de Aragua, the Sinaloa Cartel, and Los Zetas. The indictment charged Maduro and his wife with conspiring with others to import cocaine from Venezuela into the United States, and to arm drug traffickers with automatic weapons and destructive devices. The indictment further alleges that Maduro led the Cartel de los Soles, another designated foreign terrorist organization, and abused the institutions in Venezuela to illegitimately seize power and resources in support of a vast criminal operation. Maduro's crimes and other hostile actions have resulted in a predatory incursion into the United States, the destabilization of the Western Hemisphere, massive death and human suffering, and a substantial and ongoing danger posed to our Nation.

Maduro and Flores resided at a heavily fortified location beyond the ordinary reach of American law enforcement, protected by combat-equipped armed associates, and it was anticipated that they would resist apprehension by all available means. U.S. Armed Forces were therefore necessary to help effectuate their apprehension and transportation to the United States for criminal prosecution.

The actions of our Armed Forces have been directed by the President consistent with his inherent responsibility to protect U.S. citizens both at home and abroad; to take care that the laws are faithfully executed; to conduct the foreign relations of the United States; and in furtherance of U.S. national security and foreign policy interests, pursuant to his constitutional authority and as Commander in Chief and Chief Executive.

S.J. Res. 98 should be rejected, like the previously rejected Resolutions, as it once again fails to recognize the ongoing national security threats posed by the Maduro-led Cartel de los Soles and other violent drug-trafficking cartels.

If S.J. Res. 98 were presented to the President, his advisors would recommend that he veto the joint resolution.

* * * * * * *

Fact Sheet: President Donald J. Trump Safeguards Venezuelan Oil Revenue for the Good of the American and Venezuelan People
The White House
January 9, 2026

PROTECTING VENEZUELAN OIL REVENUE: Today, President Donald J. Trump signed an Executive Order declaring a national emergency to safeguard Venezuelan oil revenue held in U.S. Treasury accounts from attachment or judicial process, ensuring these funds are preserved to advance U.S. foreign policy objectives.

The Order blocks any attachment, judgment, decree, lien, execution, garnishment, or other judicial process against Foreign Government Deposit Funds.

Foreign Government Deposit Funds are defined as the Venezuelan oil revenues and diluent sales held in U.S. Treasury accounts.

The Order prohibits transfers or dealings in these funds except as authorized, superseding any prior Orders that might block or regulate them.

The Order affirms the funds are sovereign property of Venezuela held in U.S. custody for governmental and diplomatic purposes, not subject to private claims.

ADVANCING U.S. NATIONAL SECURITY AND FOREIGN POLICY: President Trump is preventing the seizure of Venezuelan oil revenue that could undermine critical U.S. efforts to ensure economic and political stability in Venezuela.

Allowing attachment of these funds would directly jeopardize U.S. objectives, including stemming the influx of illegal aliens and disrupting the flood of illicit narcotics, which has resulted in the death of countless thousands of American citizens.

Loss of control over these revenues would empower malign actors like Iran and Hezbollah while weakening efforts to bring peace, prosperity, and stability to the Venezuelan people and to the Western Hemisphere as a whole.

The funds are held solely for sovereign purposes, and their protection reinforces U.S. compliance with international obligations and supports broader diplomatic objectives.

PUTTING AMERICA FIRST: President Trump has relentlessly confronted threats from the Maduro regime and its enablers to defend American interests and strengthen hemispheric security.

On Day One, President Trump designated eight cartels, including Tren de Aragua, as Foreign Terrorist Organizations (FTOs) and Specially Designated Global Terrorists (SDGTs) to combat their violence and drug trafficking.

President Trump authorized lethal force against narcoterrorists to stop the flow of deadly drugs into this country.

President Trump ordered a total and complete blockade of all sanctioned oil tankers going in and out of Venezuela.

President Trump offered multiple off ramps to Maduro and his illegitimate regime, but he was clear that the drug trafficking must stop.

Last week, U.S. armed forces conducted an extraordinary military operation in support of law enforcement to bring outlaw dictator Maduro to justice.

It is in the national interest of the United States to be surrounded by safe, secure, and stable countries; The President's actions reestablish deterrence in the Western hemisphere.

By safeguarding these oil revenues, President Trump is promoting stability in Venezuela while advancing America First objectives: stopping illegal immigration, blocking deadly narcotics, and countering malign foreign influence.

exhibits.label: DX-1
exhibits.text:
RUBIO: This Is Our Hemisphere — and President Trump Will Not Allow Our Security to be Threatened
The White House
January 4, 2026

This morning, Secretary of State Marco Rubio joined multiple news programs to discuss the Trump Administration’s decisive operation that successfully apprehended indicted narcoterrorist and illegitimate former Venezuelan dictator Nicolás Maduro. Secretary Rubio underscored President Donald J. Trump’s ironclad commitment to preventing the Western Hemisphere from becoming a safe haven for drug traffickers, Iranian proxies, or hostile regimes that endanger our national security — declaring the days of weakness are over and the U.S. will deploy every tool to eradicate these threats from our backyard.

Material excerpts preserved from the White House release:

- “There’s not a war. We are at war against drug trafficking organizations — not a war against Venezuela.”

- “We don’t have U.S. forces on the ground in Venezuela. They were on the ground for about two hours when they went to capture Maduro… What the President is saying is very simple — and that is as President of the United States, he is not going to go around telling people what he’s not going to do.”

- “This was not an action that required congressional approval. In fact, it couldn’t require congressional approval because this was not an invasion. This is not an extended military operation… We will seek congressional approval for actions that require congressional approval. Otherwise, they’ll get congressional notification.”

- “We retain all the options we had before this raid and this capture and this arrest… until such time as changes are made.”

exhibits.label: DX-2
exhibits.text:
Remarks by Secretary of War Pete Hegseth at Newport News Shipyard
U.S. Department of War transcript
Jan. 5, 2026

Material excerpt preserved from the official transcript:

Secretary of War Pete Hegseth, discussing recent U.S. actions, stated: “And then we saw three nights ago in downtown Caracas in Venezuela, as nearly 200 of our greatest Americans went downtown in Caracas. Seems those Russian air defenses didn't quite work so well, did they? Downtown Caracas and grabbed an indicted individual wanted by American justice in support of law enforcement without a single American killed, reestablishing deterrence.”

Context preserved: the remarks compared the Caracas action with other deterrence examples and described it as American power and strength, but the material description of the Venezuelan ground action was that personnel went downtown Caracas and grabbed an indicted individual wanted by American justice in support of law enforcement.

openings.text:
Council members, the plaintiff’s burden is focused and demanding. This case is not about whether tensions existed between the United States and Venezuela, whether force was threatened, or whether a dramatic operation occurred. The proposition resolves YES only if the evidence shows that U.S. ground forces physically entered Venezuelan sovereign territory in a combat capacity and did so with one of the qualifying objectives: seizing or holding territory, removing the sitting government, or establishing operational control of a major population center.

The current record contains no admitted evidence yet. That matters. At this opening stage, the plaintiff is not asking the council to treat outside reports, search results, or counsel’s investigation as proof. The plaintiff is identifying the dispute that the admitted record must decide.

The first decisive question will be physical entry: whether official sources confirm U.S. personnel on the ground inside Venezuela, not merely aircraft, ships, cyber activity, proxy forces, or standoff strikes. The second will be combat capacity: whether the entry was part of a military operation rather than diplomacy, evacuation, or some other noncombat presence. The third, and likely hardest, will be objective. The event definition and edge cases make clear that a limited raid or capture of a head of state, standing alone, is not enough if there was no territorial occupation or operational-control objective.

That is where the source hierarchy will do real work. The strongest proof will be official statements from the Department of Defense or the White House, and, if there is a conflict between them, the fallback directs the council to UN Security Council records. The plaintiff expects the later evidence phases to turn on those official materials: what they say about U.S. ground entry, the geographic location of the operation, command and control, whether a major population center was brought under operational control, and whether the stated mission crossed the line from a targeted raid into a qualifying government-removal or territorial-control operation.

The defense will likely argue that any U.S. action was at most a targeted strike, blockade, special operations raid, or head-of-state capture without territorial occupation. That argument is serious because the proposition expressly excludes those scenarios. But it is not the end of the analysis. If official statements show that U.S. ground troops crossed into Venezuela in combat capacity and that the mission included operational control of a major population center or a qualifying government-removal objective, then the carveouts do not defeat resolution to YES.

The plaintiff’s case therefore will not rest on labels. Words such as “limited,” “raid,” or “capture” may be relevant, but they are not conclusive if the operational facts show ground entry and qualifying control or removal objectives. Conversely, rhetoric about regime change will not suffice if the official record shows only a nonqualifying extraction or strike. The council should weigh the admitted official record for what it proves, what it does not prove, and whether the source chain is complete.

For now, the plaintiff asks the council to keep the burden framed correctly: this is a preponderance inquiry into the official source record. If the admitted evidence establishes U.S. ground forces inside Venezuelan territory in combat capacity with the qualifying objective described in the proposition before the January 31, 2026 expiration, the event resolves YES.

Council members, the defense starts with the proposition’s exact limits. This case does not resolve YES merely because the United States used force near Venezuela, conducted a raid inside Venezuela, captured a Venezuelan leader, or influenced what happened afterward. The plaintiff must prove, from admitted official-source evidence, that U.S. ground troops entered Venezuelan sovereign territory in combat capacity with a qualifying objective: seizing or holding territory, removing the sitting government, or establishing operational control of a major population center.

The current record does not contain that proof. There is no admitted evidence at all: no Department of Defense or White House statement, no UN Security Council record, no transcript, no case-packet exhibit, and no authenticated source material. The plaintiff’s opening correctly recognizes that the burden will turn on the admitted official record. That burden matters because the event definition is intentionally narrower than a general “U.S. operation in Venezuela” question.

The defense theory is straightforward. The proposition expressly excludes targeted strikes, blockades, cyber operations, proxy conduct, special operations raids without territorial objectives, and, most importantly here, capture of a head of state without territorial occupation. That edge case is not decorative. It prevents the council from converting a dramatic capture or extraction into a YES unless the plaintiff also proves the required territorial, governmental-removal, or major-population-center control objective.

The later evidence phase should therefore focus on four questions. First, what official source actually confirms: ground entry, timing, personnel, location, combat capacity, and mission character. Second, whether the official materials describe an occupation or operational control of a major population center, as opposed to a short-duration entry to seize a person. Third, whether any language about political transition or pressure is an operational objective of U.S. ground forces, rather than rhetoric or diplomacy after the fact. Fourth, whether the Department of Defense, White House, and UN records are consistent; if they conflict, the proposition itself directs attention to the UN Security Council record.

The defense has already identified the official-source categories that must be tested when admission opens: Defense or White House material addressing any alleged Caracas operation, any White House explanation of whether forces remained on the ground or whether the action was an invasion or extended operation, and Security Council coverage if there is a source conflict. Those materials may contain language each side will want to use. But until admitted, they are not record proof; and once admitted, they must be read for operational facts, not headlines.

The plaintiff may try to argue that removing Maduro from custody or power automatically satisfies the proposition. The text says otherwise. A capture of a head of state without territorial occupation resolves NO. So the decisive issue will be the missing link: did U.S. ground troops enter Venezuela to occupy, hold, remove the government as an operational military objective, or control a major population center, or did they conduct a limited raid or extraction that the proposition excludes?

On this record, the council should hold the plaintiff to that line. If later admitted official evidence proves only a temporary ground raid, a law-enforcement-style capture, a strike package, political pressure, or post-operation transition talk without territorial occupation or operational control, the event resolves NO. The defense will ask the council to weigh provenance, source hierarchy, and exact wording, and to reject any attempt to fill the proposition’s required objective with inference alone.

prior_council_votes.rationale: The evidence shows U.S. ground forces entered Venezuela and operated in combat capacity with objectives that included removal of Maduro and oversight of the transition, satisfying the proposition's requirements.

rebuttals.text:
Council members, the defense argument succeeds only if the proposition is rewritten to require an invasion, extended occupation, or permanent troop presence. It does not. It requires U.S. ground troops entering Venezuelan territory in combat capacity with a qualifying objective.

The defendant’s new evidence concedes the entry element. DX-1 says U.S. forces “were on the ground for about two hours when they went to capture Maduro.” DX-2 says nearly 200 Americans went “downtown Caracas” and grabbed Maduro. Those are official confirmations of U.S. personnel on Venezuelan soil in Caracas. The defense also does not seriously dispute combat capacity: the mission involved nearly 200 personnel downtown Caracas, Russian air defenses, a fortified target, and military force in support of law enforcement.

The defense’s real point is objective. But “not an invasion” and “not an extended military operation” are not dispositive terms in this proposition. A two-hour operation can still satisfy the event if its objective included removing the sitting government or establishing operational control. The proposition’s edge case excludes capture of a head of state without territorial occupation; it does not say every brief capture is automatically NO regardless of surrounding official statements.

That is why PX-1 remains decisive. PX-1 does not merely say Maduro was arrested. It says Maduro was “now out of power,” that the U.S. would “oversee Venezuela” until a safe transition could be identified, and that U.S. officials were working directly with the successor on what the U.S. thought necessary. Those statements supply the missing objective: removal from power and operational control over the transition. DX-1’s statement that the United States retained options “until such time as changes are made” fits that same coercive/control posture.

The defense also overreads PX-2. The SAP’s law-enforcement framing explains why Maduro was seized and transported, but it also confirms hostilities within Venezuela and that U.S. Armed Forces were necessary because ordinary law enforcement could not reach the fortified target and resistance was anticipated. The label “law enforcement” does not negate the official record of military ground entry and government-removal/transition-control objectives.

So the council should accept the defense’s admissions but reject its legal shortcut. The official record proves ground troops in Caracas. It proves a combat-capable operation. And, through PX-1’s out-of-power and U.S.-oversight statements, it proves more than a standalone head-of-state capture. On a preponderance, the event resolves YES.

surrebuttals.text:
Council members, plaintiff’s rebuttal usefully narrows the case. Entry and combat capacity are not the defense’s main ground. The unresolved element is objective, and plaintiff still has not connected the two-hour capture operation to territorial occupation or operational control.

The defense is not asking the council to add requirements like “permanent occupation” or “extended invasion.” The defense is asking the council to enforce the requirements already in the proposition. A head-of-state capture is expressly NO when it lacks territorial occupation. A special operations raid is NO when it lacks territorial objectives. That means there must be some additional operational fact beyond “U.S. forces captured Maduro and Maduro was then out of power.” Otherwise the edge case disappears: every successful capture of a sitting head of state would become a qualifying removal simply because the captured person no longer governs from the palace.

Plaintiff’s rebuttal points to DX-1 and DX-2 as admissions. They are admissions of the limited operation, not admissions of a qualifying objective. DX-1 says U.S. forces were on the ground for about two hours to capture Maduro; it says the action was not an invasion and not an extended military operation; it calls the event a raid, capture, and arrest. DX-2 says nearly 200 Americans went downtown Caracas and grabbed an indicted individual wanted by American justice in support of law enforcement. Those words are strong evidence of the excluded scenario: a brief armed capture raid.

PX-2 remains the most precise official statement of objective. It says the Armed Forces acted in furtherance of a law-enforcement operation to apprehend and transport Maduro and Flores to the United States for federal criminal prosecution, and that military force was necessary to effectuate apprehension and transportation because ordinary law enforcement could not reach them. That is not a description of seizing territory, holding territory, replacing a government by force, or exercising operational control over Caracas.

Plaintiff’s remaining point is PX-1’s “out of power” and “oversee Venezuela” language. But PX-1 does not say U.S. ground troops stayed, held ground, controlled Caracas, controlled Venezuelan institutions, or made the succession effective by military occupation. It says Vice President Delcy Rodriguez was “just sworn in” and willing to work with the United States. That is a political aftermath and diplomatic leverage point, not proof that the ground force had the qualifying objective when it entered.

PX-3 is still farther away: custody of oil revenues and foreign-policy objectives are not territorial occupation or operational control of a major population center. The proposition is about a military operation involving ground troops, not every later instrument of U.S. influence.

So the council can accept plaintiff’s strongest facts and still resolve NO. U.S. forces entered Caracas in a short, combat-capable capture mission. The official record then describes apprehension, transport, arrest, prosecution, and law-enforcement support. It does not prove the additional territorial or operational-control objective that the proposition requires and the edge case demands.

technical_reports.summary: Record and evidence list were checked before filing; no case-packet evidence was visible. Plaintiff searched official War.gov/DoD, White House, and UN-related sources. War.gov direct curl returned HTTP 403, but the official page was rendered through web.open and submitted as PX-1. The White House SAP PDF was downloaded locally (159,669 bytes; SHA-256 ee2cf252a56c4c345e913bc8dde77640b6b930e095b7488d1906d52ec4b70678), but local PDF text tools/pip were unavailable, so readable PDF text from web.open was submitted as PX-2. The White House fact sheet HTML was retrieved and text-extracted with Python HTMLParser, then submitted as PX-3. A UN press-release lead was found, but local retrieval produced a client challenge and no source text was submitted; UN records would matter most if the official U.S. sources conflict.
technical_reports.title: Plaintiff Source Search and Custody Ledger

technical_reports.summary: Before filing, defense inspected the live record and evidence list, then used stat_evidence and read_evidence_range on all three plaintiff exhibits. PX-1 proves a ground-based extraction force in Caracas but also frames the action as a raid/extraction. PX-2 expressly frames the action as targeted and limited strikes in furtherance of apprehension and transport for prosecution. PX-3 concerns post-operation oil-revenue custody and states the operation supported law enforcement. Defense submitted two additional official sources: DX-1, a White House Rubio release stating U.S. forces were on the ground about two hours to capture Maduro and that the action was not an invasion or extended military operation; and DX-2, a Department of War transcript describing the action as grabbing an indicted individual wanted by American justice in support of law enforcement. A UN press-release lead was attempted but not captured in usable form; no UN record is admitted.
technical_reports.title: Defense Source Search and Evidence Review Ledger

technical_reports.summary: Plaintiff inspected the live record and evidence list after defendant merits, then read the full text of DX-1 and DX-2 with read_evidence_range. The rebuttal uses those defendant sources as admissions of U.S. forces physically on the ground in Venezuela, nearly 200 Americans downtown Caracas, and a continuing posture until changes were made. No new outside evidence was needed because the issue is the legal effect of existing official-source admissions and plaintiff PX-1.
